item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Even though it will never get tagged as such, I feel this article fits well in the Site-17 Catalogue canon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like the idea and the way the story progresses in this article. However, I do agree with the post CarrieVS made about the man coming back to life. This happens more often than many may think. With this said, there should be a change to why The Foundation became involved. But nonetheless, I still enjoyed this SCP article.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have issues with the first interview. The doctor stammering instead of coming up with an immediate response is just unprofessional-sounding, along with the interview cutting off because he wanted a promise his family wouldn't be told is unnecessary. They were already given fake ashes, and even if they weren't given them, why would the Foundation ever let them know about what really happened? There's no real issue here, it's just a needless cutoff for drama.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh thank god, I thought it was a racial slur. But the keeping tabs thing is more what I meant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A niggle is a little thing that annoys/bugs (or 'niggles at') you.

No, it's not strange enough. It's rare, sure, but a perfectly well-documented non-anomalous phenomenon. If it happened more than once to the same person, sure. Or if there was a statistically remarkable number of cases somewhere, or some other unusual circumstance amounting to at least some evidence that it's something other than the afore-mentioned non-anomalous phenomenon. But I just don't buy that the Foundation acts on every incidence of someone turning out not to be dead after all.

It would be plausible if they monitored reports of people reviving after being pronounced dead, maybe if they routinely kept tabs on such people. But I can't make the 'Recovery' paragraph read any other way than 'immediately, on no other grounds than his having revived after being pronounced dead, detained him, faked his death, and erased the memories of all other people involved'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't see why we wouldn't get involved anyway. That kind of thing is strange enough that we should check them out just in case IMHO.

Also, what the hell is a niggle?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I kinda like this, might have upvoted if it wasn't for a little niggle that, for me, spoils it.

I find it a bit implausible that the Foundation immediately got involved when a guy in a morgue regains consciousness. These things happen occasionally.

I feel like it should have happened to the guy more than once before the Foundation would take an interest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's a lot of potential here, but something just feels…off. I'm not sure. Neutral vote for now; I like the idea of someone dying and coming back piecemeal, though.

item.title: Re: Anagrams?
item.content:encoded:
Nice catch, enjoy your easter egg!

On another note, I'm going to clean this article up a bit. It's been sitting at a fairly decent +40, so it hasn't been my top priority just yet, but it will probably be updated at some point.

Thanks for the comments everyone, they have been taken into consideration in the cleanup.

item.title: Re: Anagrams?
item.content:encoded: Oh my god

item.title: Anagrams?
item.content:encoded: Dharow and Ipps-Hill… Howard and Phillips? Clever girl.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this, however,

Subject's concerns are to be disregarded.

Obvious statement is obvious

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My complaint is similar, the foundation has show itself to be adept at thew interrogation of unwilling subject over and over. It also has many passive SCP objects that could do the mind reading to find out if the guy is telling the truth on weather or not he knows what is going on. Putting that aside however, I do like the overall tone and style of the piece, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I understand that, but I'm not sure. The doctor's tone just felt off to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I talk how I write… but then again, I don't write real clinical.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's an interview. Of course it's not clinical. Most people don't talk like how they write.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like the last two interview logs should be scrapped, while the first three should be revised for a note clinical tone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There is way too little text under each collapsible to justify them being there. Overall, it just feels kinda eh without it really going anywhere interesting, as well having tone problems.

item.title: Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
This article is up for review. Check it out, we feel it needs more attention.

I liked where this was initially going with the interviews, but they just became too cliche' for me by the end. I also agree with the sentiment that the Foundation doesn't seem to be acting competent here. I think that adjusting the last three interviews, maybe even scrapping the last one, would do this one well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel the Foundation is being a bit too dense here. I see nothing to indicate that they have any reason to think the man knows the reason for his anomaly, nor any reason to expect to find out more. Without any prospect for improvement, why wouldn't they comply with his request for cremation? It's more likely to produce results than another ten repetitions of the dying and reanimating.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you. Nice to read someone's interpretation of this, I appreciate it.
You're not far off from what I based this on. It's the idea that mind and matter are two distinct substances, but they are tied together. That even when parts of your body are removed, they don't just go away, but go to this limbo to wait to be rejoined with the rest of you for the afterlife. The "soul" of the man goes there as well when he dies, to be put "whole" again, but it never really happens, because he is pulled back from there as he comes back to life. He believes the only way to break the bond is to completely destroy the body. Of course, we don't know if that would work either. His belief is based on this (italics mine):

Forasmuch as it hath pleased Almighty God of his great mercy to take unto himself the soul of our dear brother here departed, we therefore commit his body to the ground; earth to earth, ashes to ashes, dust to dust; in sure and certain hope of the Resurrection to eternal life, through our Lord Jesus Christ; who shall change our vile body, that it may be like unto his glorious body, according to the mighty working, whereby he is able to subdue all things to himself.

Could be that it's just this man because of his unique condition, could be that it's happening to everyone, but he's the only one to know about it, because he comes back. Just think of how much has been removed of your body. Hair, nails, baby teeth. If there is an afterlife, it might not be exactly like people think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The thing that really made this for me was the description of the "waiting room". That, combined with his desire to be burned, made me think that the reason he's immortal is that his soul (or spirit, or consciousness, or whatever) never leaves his body. The hell, or limbo, or whatever, is just his old, slowly decaying body, and he wants to be burned so it can leave. That's what I got out of it at least. Also, the idea that this is what happens to everyone (as some of the notes seem to imply) really creeped me out. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have cleaned up the Procedures and Description a bit, I noticed some repetition here and there etc.
I hope I made it a little less rambling. Let me know if this changes the tone for better or worse. Or at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And, upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Some changes made on directly stated smaller issues.

I have to say I didn't expect this to do as well as it has now and I appreciate the feedback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll fix that, thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think it works better if that's just implied, but I'll keep that in mind.

The way I see it, they just leave him to die every time. That's pretty much the only way they can observe or study the anomaly. As they don't know how the anomaly really works, I think they wouldn't try to interfere with it, but if they would try to prevent it, I suppose death would take its course anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good suggestions, thanks. I'm putting those in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, that makes sense. Thanks. I'm working on changes now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No worries. Your comment sparked that picture and that fantastic little piece of dialogue, so we're all good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm digging the gown.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted for the interaction between the patient and the doctor.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't like this, I get the concept and I read the interviews but it does nothing for me. There is not much here besides an old man who escapes death and wants to be burned.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't really find this interesting in the least, but not poor enough for a downvote either. The entity responsible for this post has been terminated. I'm wondering why the 'o' in the title is italicized. It's an intriguing aspect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The subject

the subject's

Subject

The subject

Way too much repetition of "the subject" and variations of. It made it a bit of a pain to read through the description. Use more "SCP-1922."

As for the rest..eh…no vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with ChazzK in switching the second and third paragraphs in the description-I think it would be clearer. In the present description's first paragraph, instead of "gangrenes" I would say "several episodes of gangrene". In the second paragraph, instead of "Compared to subject's old age" I would think something like "Compared to the subject's advanced age". Just my suggestions.

No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The interviews salvaged this into a no-vote for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There must be a tale that incorporates this dialog.
The will of the cosmos cries out for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That he did.
http://www.srcf.ucam.org/~dm510/cheatingdeath.jpg

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah it's my fault. I should've expanded more. It wasn't a bad SCP, and could coexist nicely. Just a thought on it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 1440 did cheat them at cards.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think you need to play more off the idea that he's being taken piece by piece every time he dies.

Also does the doctor ever attempt to prevent 1922's death or is he just left to die every time?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It didn't stop me from up lying this article, it's good. Just the concept is similar. And could be an interesting crossover piece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thirded. The tone wavered a bit in the main body, and it didn't interest me much, but the interviews were a good shot-by-shot reflection of a descent into depression and despair. I enjoyed the light hint of supernatural interference without some big reveal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1440: "You know, if you stay near me for too long, you'll die."
1922: "No worries, mate."
1440: "Really? Are you not afraid of death?"
1922: "Afraid of it? Hardly. The only thing I'm afraid of is the booze running out."
1440: "The booze is man-made, so it will also be destroyed eventually."
1922: "I don't think we can be friends anymore."
Death: "I hate you guys."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh yes! One suggestion though: instead of nearby, put the reaper at the table as well. I mean, if they can cheat him at life, I'm pretty sure they cheat him at cards too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now i'm thinking the soldier and this guy playing cards, drinking booze, and grim reaper nearby having a hissy fit.
Someone should draw it.. hell, even I might.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this one. My main suggestion to you would be to post the thing that makes him anomalous at, or near, the top of the description; in this case I'd say swap the second and third paragraphs, because as I went along I was starting to think that this guy was anomalous because he was… Old and sick but not dead yet. Which would have been infinitely more boring.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for that. Hopefully I can pull the rest of the article to the same level at some point.

The man has been there for years and the Foundation get next to nothing out of him, so I think even they'd find it sometimes hard to stay professional. But I guess I could tone it down, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 1440 was posted at the time when I was still drafting this and yes, I was concerned that this would be compared to it. I felt it was dealing with the same theme, but in a completely different way, so I decided to give this one a go anyhow. Dmatix himself put it better than I could've. The similarity between the men is that they both have found a way to cheat death and both pay a heavy price for that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You mean 1440? It's not really that similar. 1440 is incredibly destructive, while this one is harmless, 1440 is undying while this one resurrects himself, and 1440's body is indestructible while this one degrades. They both cheated death in very different ways, and both suffer for it differently- in 1440's case it was hubris, and the suffering he suffers is of loneliness and guilt, of seeking death and never finding it, while 1922 reason for undeath is unknown, and is more about physical pain and the terror of constant death without end. I think they could co-exist.

As for the article itself, I feel about it much like the others- the interviews are good, but the body of the article is lacking. Neutral vote for the time being.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is similar to that one Dmatix(?) wrote, but not as good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted so people will read the interviews. However:

We don't know and you're not really helping, are you now?

The dick move on the doctor's part breaks the tone and subsequently my suspension of disbelief.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you all for your feedback. My intention was to make the object a bit boring and act more as a vehicle for the story. I understand though if I made the article less interesting that way and I probably have not pulled it off too well. Anyway, I appreciate your words and I'm happy to know I've managed to do at least something right.

I'll try to think of a way to improve the rest of the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The interviews are good, but the rest is rambling and incoherent. I couldn't tell what the SCP was supposed to be from the description. Neutral vote for now.

Edit: That's significantly better. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is pretty much how I feel. I'm not particularly thrilled by the object, but those interviews do enough to keep me neutral.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh.
I have issues with tone in this one, and it's not that much of a novel idea, but then, the interviews are good, and sort of outbalance the suck.
Like, all the "You are cutting off rotting bits off me, and keep me here, and don't even give me a fucking pack of fags. Why should I cooperate, not like there's anything worse you can do." attitude just makes sense and fits well, and the whole interview pack feels believable.
No vote, but good job there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
New SCP, somewhat inspired by Bob Marley and my late grandpa collectively. Fire away.
Also, not sure what tags to add beside the obvious, so please do add more if needed.
